The Wages of Fear

(six-month salary bonus to cross the Strait of Hormuz into the Persian Gulf — and back)

Lionheart Capital is close to a $400 million deal for a stake in a Venezuelan oil field, in what would be one of the first major private investments in the country’s oil industry since the US began easing sanctions.

Miami-based Lionheart signed a non-binding letter of intent with Swedish firm Keo Capital AB, which owns a stake in PetroUrdaneta, according to a statement released Monday.

Under the proposed deal, a new company owned by Keo and Lionheart would be formed and traded on the Nasdaq. The parties are negotiating and aim to execute a final agreement by Aug. 17, Keo said in the statement.
#oott
https://t.co/hzOqtgjeHz

Still playing with this data, so please share sources if you've got any favourites on hand.

No doubt Chinese EV penetration is skyrocketing and it's going to be a growing issue for future oil balances, but still not convinced it was a major driver of post-Hormuz import pullback.

China typically buys ~75% of Omani crude oil exports

Omani exports weren't materially disrupted by the Iran War/Hormuz stoppage, yet Omani crude sales to China collapsed to functionally nothing by in June, freeing up Omani barrels for Indian, Taiwanese, & South Korean importers https://t.co/uP3FknVRcA

This is a pretty good illustration of the fact that China's buyer's strike wasn't hasn't been simply about not replacing lost Hormuz-flowing barrels, but rather a broader collapse in crude demand by Chinese refineries.

(data via @Kpler)
Tanker strike reported the Strait of Hormuz https://t.co/AsCvNumoK4
